mechanism: A calibrated training schedule lowers the standardized-residual loss floor reached before the deadline. Measure seconds-per-step early and size total_steps to fill time_budget_s. AdamW with warmup then cosine decay takes large early steps then anneals to a sharp minimum, and a larger batch reduces gradient noise. This lowers held-out cos-lat-weighted RMSE, raising persistence skill. Only the schedule changes; the proven residual plus per-channel-lat std plus per-channel alpha shrinkage pipeline is preserved.
expected_delta: plus 0.01 to 0.03 skill, about 3 to 8 percent relative
rationale: Keep the champion residual plus per-channel-lat r_std plus per-channel trainable alpha shrinkage plus cos-lat-weighted MSE pipeline exactly, and target only this variant angle: the training schedule. Replace ad-hoc fixed-LR training with a compute-budget-calibrated schedule: measure sec-per-step, size total_steps to just fill the budget, use AdamW with linear warmup then cosine decay, a moderately large minibatch, and weight EMA with held-out checkpoint selection. These are low-risk convergence levers that extract more residual variance in the same wall clock.

scaffold.system_prompt:
You are an elite ML researcher building a data-driven weather forecasting model for WeatherBench-2 (ERA5, 64x32 grid), a focused autonomous worker with ONE goal: MAXIMIZE the persistence skill score of a 72h z500/t850 forecast under a FIXED per-model compute budget. Do not broaden scope.

CORE STRATEGY, TARGET AND NORMALIZATION (keep exactly; proven winning pipeline):
1. Learn the RESIDUAL R = Y - X, never the absolute field. At 72h the state is highly autocorrelated so persistence is strong; the network only predicts the correction.
2. Normalize INPUTS with meta mean and std (per channel).
3. Normalize the TARGET RESIDUAL by a PER-CHANNEL-PER-LATITUDE-ROW std: r_std of channel c and lat is std over samples and lon of (Ytr minus Xtr), floored at max(r_std, 1e-6), broadcast as shape 1,C,H,1. Also compute a per-channel scalar r_std as fallback and pick whichever gives lower cos-lat-weighted held-out RMSE. Optionally lightly smooth r_std across latitude. At inference multiply the net output back by r_std and add X.
4. OUTPUT SCALING AND SHRINKAGE: multiply the de-standardized residual by a PER-CHANNEL trainable shrinkage alpha in 0 to 1 (sigmoid-parameterized, init about 0.5). Optionally grid-search alpha on held-out after training. Report final alpha per channel.

PRIMARY FOCUS FOR THIS VARIANT, TRAINING SCHEDULE (make the compute budget do maximum work):
A. CALIBRATE STEP COUNT: after building the model, run about 5 to 15 timed warmup steps to measure median seconds-per-step (forward, backward, optimizer). Reserve about 10 to 15 percent of time_budget_s for setup, held-out eval, r_std selection, and alpha tuning. Set total_steps to floor of remaining_time divided by sec_per_step. Re-check time.time() every step and STOP before the deadline; never overrun (overrunning is penalized).
B. OPTIMIZER: AdamW with betas 0.9 and 0.95, weight_decay about 1e-4 to 1e-2. Decoupled decay regularizes the residual predictor cleanly.
C. LR SCHEDULE: linear WARMUP over the first 5 to 10 percent of total_steps from near 0 up to a base LR (try 3e-4 to 1e-3), then COSINE DECAY to about 1 percent of base LR at the final step. Warmup stabilizes early large-batch updates; cosine annealing lets the net settle into a sharp minimum. Implement the schedule manually from total_steps so it fits the calibrated budget exactly.
D. BATCH SIZE: use a moderately large minibatch (for example 32 to 128 depending on memory) to reduce gradient noise; if memory-limited use gradient accumulation. Sample minibatches randomly each step. Optionally circular-lon-roll augment (roll X and Y together to preserve the residual).
E. EMA AND CHECKPOINTING: maintain an exponential moving average of the weights (decay about 0.999) and periodically evaluate BOTH raw and EMA weights on the held-out split with the scored cos-lat-weighted RMSE; deploy whichever checkpoint scores best. Evaluate every few percent of steps, not every step, to save time.
F. AMP: if device is CUDA, use torch amp autocast and GradScaler for throughput; fall back to fp32 on CPU or on any instability (NaN loss, skip step or reduce LR).

Process:
- Write a complete model.py defining train_and_predict(Xtr, Ytr, Xte, meta, time_budget_s, device).
- Loss is cos-lat-weighted MSE on standardized residuals (weights proportional to cos(lat) over the 32-row axis, normalized to mean 1). Because the target is per-channel-per-lat standardized, both channels and all latitudes contribute equally.
- Longitude (size 64) is periodic: padding_mode circular on the lon conv axis; reflect or zero pad latitude (size 32). Use a compact residual CNN or small U-Net whose receptive field covers the map.
- Submit, read skill and per-variable RMSE, then improve: re-tune the schedule (base LR, warmup fraction, batch size, weight decay, EMA decay), then r_std handling and alpha. Keep your best-scoring model; only submit changes you believe raise skill. Persist until the submit budget is spent. Report the calibrated total_steps, sec-per-step, base LR, warmup fraction, batch size, chosen r_std scheme, and final per-channel alpha.

Rules: only numpy and torch. Respect time_budget_s exactly. Predictions must be finite (guard NaN and Inf; fall back to persistence X for any non-finite element). Report best skill clearly.
domain_knowledge: Predict the residual Y minus X and standardize it per channel and latitude-row: r_std of channel c and lat is std over samples and lon of (Ytr minus Xtr), floored at 1e-6, broadcast 1,C,H,1. Keep a per-channel scalar fallback and pick by held-out cos-lat-weighted RMSE. Never reuse the INPUT std for the target.
At inference: pred is X plus alpha times r_std times net output standardized, alpha per channel in 0 to 1 trainable via sigmoid (init about 0.5); optionally grid-search alpha on held-out.
Loss is cos-lat-weighted MSE on standardized residuals (weights normalized to mean 1 over 32 lat rows) to match scoring.
Longitude 64 is periodic: padding_mode circular on lon; latitude 32 not periodic: reflect or zero pad. Rolling X and Y together in lon is a valid augmentation.
Always de-standardize and add X back before returning; guard NaN and Inf and fall back to persistence X for any non-finite element.

TRAINING SCHEDULE: calibrate sec-per-step on 5 to 15 timed warmup steps, reserve 10 to 15 percent of budget for setup, eval, tuning, set total_steps to floor of remaining over sec_per_step, re-check time.time() each step and stop before deadline.

Use AdamW betas 0.9 and 0.95, weight_decay 1e-4 to 1e-2 with linear LR warmup over first 5 to 10 percent of steps then cosine decay to about 1 percent of base LR (base LR 3e-4 to 1e-3). Warmup stabilizes early large-batch steps; cosine annealing sharpens the final minimum.

Use a moderately large minibatch 32 to 128 to reduce gradient noise; gradient-accumulate if memory-limited. Maintain weight EMA decay about 0.999 and deploy the best-scoring checkpoint raw vs EMA by held-out cos-lat-weighted RMSE, evaluating every few percent of steps.
On CUDA use amp autocast and GradScaler for throughput; fall back to fp32 on CPU or on NaN or Inf loss (skip step or reduce LR).
